beliefs.title: Three things we believe. Three things we're building.
beliefs.lead: We're not fighting anything. We're building what we wish already existed.

rows.belief: Engineers shouldn't do by hand what a machine can do. Routine is a quiet tax on the craft.
rows.answerLead: One engine for releases, reviews, QA gates, and agent pipelines. The scripts you keep rewriting — expressed once, observed fully.

rows.belief: Internal platforms should be open. The tools that make engineering teams strong don't need to live behind closed doors.
rows.answerLead: Everything is a plugin. Every plugin is code you can read, fork, and extend. No black boxes.

rows.belief: Your stack should stay yours. Switching a broker, a database, or an LLM shouldn't mean touching every service you own.
rows.answerLead: 25+ contract interfaces between your code and your infrastructure. Swap what's behind the contract. The code stays still.

sameRails.title: Same rails. Humans, agents, whatever comes next.
sameRails.lead: A platform is a set of rules and a runtime that enforces them. KB Labs applies the same rails to everyone who runs inside it — you, your teammates, internal plugin-agents, external agents like Claude or Codex, CI bots, future MCP connections. One permissions model, one observability surface, one extension contract.

points.title: One permissions model.
points.description: Everyone who runs declares what they're allowed to do. The runtime enforces it the same way for everyone.

points.title: One observability surface.
points.description: Every run is traced — who started it, what it touched, what came out. Humans and agents show up in the same log.

points.title: One extension contract.
points.description: Humans write plugins. Agents write plugins. Anyone extends the platform through the same interfaces, with the same governance.

sameRails.caption: A person plus agents is a team. KB Labs treats them that way from day one. MCP is on the roadmap — as a protocol-level extension of the same model, not a bolt-on.

startBeside.title: Start beside your current stack. Not instead of it.

items.q: We already have a full stack. Do we need to migrate?
items.a: No. KB Labs is designed to sit beside what you already run. Install it, connect one workflow, and keep everything else untouched. You grow the surface area when it makes sense for your team — never because the tool asked you to.

items.q: Is it free? Will it stay free?
items.a: The OSS core is free and will stay free. It's the engine, and the engine doesn't close. A commercial support layer is on the roadmap for teams that need guarantees — SLA, dedicated support, advanced governance — but it's additive, never a gate in front of the core.

items.q: I work alone, or with a tiny team. Is this still for me?
items.a: Especially for you. KB Labs is built on the idea that a person plus agents is a team. A plugin-agent can run your reviews. An external agent like Claude or Codex can drive your workflows through the same contracts. You stay in control of what they're allowed to do. Solo builders with agents get the most value from this from day one.

items.q: How does KB Labs treat humans, agents, and bots?
items.a: The same way. KB Labs is a runtime with one permissions model, one observability surface, and one extension contract — applied equally to any kind of runner. A human running a workflow, an internal plugin-agent, an external agent, a CI bot — they all declare what they're allowed to do and the runtime enforces it. No one gets privileges. No one escapes the rails. MCP is on the roadmap as the next protocol-level extension of this.

items.q: How long does on-prem setup actually take?
items.a: One command installs the launcher. A working environment with your first workflow is typically ready within an hour. No cloud dependency, no provisioning ceremony.

items.q: We already have CI/CD. Why not just extend that?
items.a: CI/CD is great at build and deploy. KB Labs is for everything around it — commit policy, AI review gates, QA trend tracking, release coordination, agent workflows. Things that don't belong in a pipeline YAML and don't scale as config.

items.q: What does building a plugin look like?
items.a: A plugin is a TypeScript package with a manifest. You declare capabilities, write handlers, and the runtime enforces what the plugin is allowed to do. Most teams ship a working plugin in a day.

items.q: How do you handle security and permissions?
items.a: Every plugin — and every runner — declares what it's allowed to do. The runtime enforces those boundaries: nothing runs outside its declared capabilities. Every run is traced end-to-end. Full details on /security.

useCases.title: Sound familiar?
useCases.description: Six scenarios where teams replaced duct-tape automation with a single controlled platform.

items.title: Release Orchestration
items.hook: Your releases live in five Slack threads, three scripts, and a prayer.
items.situation: Every release is a manual coordination exercise — CI jobs, chat messages, local scripts, people who might be on leave.
items.how: tests → checks → AI review → QA gate → final approve. One declarative workflow. Plugins enforce each step.
items.result: Release cycle: days → minutes. Every step traced. No one skips the gate.

items.title: QA Regressions and Trends
items.hook: You find out about the regression after the customer does.
items.situation: Quality signals are scattered — lint here, tests there, trends nowhere. Issues surface post-release when remediation costs the most.
items.how: A QA plugin runs recurring checks on every change, tracks package-level trends, and surfaces degradation signals before they ship.
items.result: Catch regressions at commit time, not in production. Remediation cost drops significantly.

items.title: Commit and Change Policy
items.hook: Your commit history is a graveyard of 'fix', 'wip', and 'asdf'.
items.situation: Commit discipline lives in a Confluence page nobody reads. Enforcement depends on who reviews and whether they care that day.
items.how: A commit plugin enforces policy at the workflow level — message format, scope, type — declared once, applied everywhere.
items.result: Consistent change history across the team. Fewer manual fixups before release.

items.title: AI Review Gate
items.hook: Code review is your slowest and most inconsistent step.
items.situation: Review throughput is a bottleneck. Quality bar varies by reviewer, by day, by team. Some PRs get ten comments, others slip through.
items.how: AI review runs as a required pipeline stage with declarative policy — not a suggestion, a gate. Configurable per repo.
items.result: Faster cycle times. A consistent quality baseline that doesn't depend on who's available.

items.title: Monorepo Dependency Ops
items.hook: Your monorepo changes are a game of 'hope nothing breaks'.
items.situation: Dependency updates, package sequencing, and governance live in ad-hoc scripts and tribal knowledge. Every big change is a gamble.
items.how: Plugins handle dependency operations, build sequencing, and package governance — declaratively, with change tracking.
items.result: Predictable monorepo changes. CI glue code replaced by observable, repeatable workflows.

items.title: Internal Agent Workflows
items.hook: Your AI agents are ungoverned scripts running on someone's laptop.
items.situation: Agent scenarios are bolted onto separate stacks with no unified policy, no audit trail, and no way to enforce security boundaries.
items.how: Agents run as plugins inside the same policy-first runtime as your standard workflows — same governance, same observability.
items.result: One controlled system for agent automation. No separate stack to maintain or secure.

meta.title: Why KB Labs — Honest Comparison
meta.description: How KB Labs compares to GitHub Actions, n8n, Temporal, and AI agent frameworks. An honest answer for every alternative.

hero.title: How KB Labs compares.
hero.subtitle: We're not for everyone. Here's an honest answer for the most common alternatives — including the cases where the right answer is something else.

alternatives.label: GitHub Actions / GitLab CI
alternatives.title: CI tools run on their infrastructure. Your secrets live on their servers.
body: GitHub Actions and GitLab CI are good at deploying code — that's what they were built for. But your workflows run on their infrastructure. Your secrets, your logs, your artifacts, your timing data — all of it passes through someone else's machines.

KB Labs runs where you decide: on your server, in your VPC, on your laptop. Nothing leaves unless you choose. For teams who care about data residency, compliance, or simply don't want a third party in the loop — that's the difference.

alternatives.label: n8n / Make / Zapier
alternatives.title: Low-code is fast to start. Also fast to become unmanageable.
body: Logic lives in a GUI, not in your repo. There's no type safety, no code review process, no way to test before it runs in production. When something breaks, it often breaks silently.

KB Labs treats automation as code: typed plugins, version-controlled workflows, approval gates before anything runs. You can review it, test it, and reason about it — the same way you reason about the rest of your stack.

alternatives.label: Temporal / Prefect / Airflow
alternatives.title: Excellent tools for distributed systems. KB Labs solves a different problem.
body: If you're orchestrating microservices at scale, managing complex distributed state, or running data pipelines — Temporal and Prefect are serious tools built for that. Use them.

KB Labs is for development teams who want to automate their work: code reviews, releases, quality checks, AI-assisted workflows. No distributed systems expertise required, no infrastructure to operate.
alternatives.note: Different tools for different problems. We don't compete — we complement.

alternatives.label: LangChain / custom AI agents
alternatives.title: Agents without structure are prototypes. Useful in demos, fragile in production.
body: Most AI agent setups end up as brittle glue code — no isolation, no audit trail, no consistent way to connect your real tools. Every new capability means another custom integration.

KB Labs gives agents the same typed plugin system that human workflows use — with permissions, observability, and direct access to your codebase, docs, and internal tools. No glue code. MCP support on the roadmap.

meta.title: About — KB Labs
meta.description: KB Labs builds open developer infrastructure — AI orchestration, workflows, gateway — that you own, control, and can replace. No platform rent. No vendor lock.

hero.title: Open the closed
hero.subtitle: Workflow engine, AI gateway, plugin system — running on your machine. Swap any component with a config line.

founder.label: Founder
founder.name: Kirill Baranov
founder.bio: Frontend lead who built an AI review tool overnight in response to a CTO decision — and couldn't stop. Building KB Labs in public since 2025.

manifesto: Every vendor locks you in gradually. It starts with a smooth onboarding, competitive pricing, great integrations — until the day switching costs more than staying. That's not a technical problem. It's a business model.

The second problem is less obvious. Large companies have entire engineering teams building internal developer platforms — AI orchestration, workflow engines, API gateways, observability stacks. All of that makes everything else faster. Small teams don't have that. They piece together SaaS subscriptions, each one owning another slice of the stack, each one extracting rent.

KB Labs is the answer to both. Not another SaaS layer on top of AI APIs — an infrastructure layer you install, configure, and own. One that was designed from the start to be replaced at every boundary without a migration project.

Open the closed is not a tagline. It's a design requirement: every system boundary must be open for inspection, replacement, and extension. The concrete implementation is always a detail. The typed contract is what lasts.

principles.title: The boundary is enforced by the build
principles.body: A plugin cannot import an adapter — the build fails. That's not a convention or a code review note: adding `@kb-labs/adapter-redis` to a plugin's dependencies violates the layer graph and breaks the build. The contract is the only connection point.

principles.title: Your code. Your data.
principles.body: KB Labs runs on your infrastructure. No cloud backend owned by a vendor, no data leaving your perimeter unless you explicitly route it. On-prem and air-gapped by design.

principles.title: 12 contracts across the platform
principles.body: ILLM, ICache, IStorage, IVectorStore, IEventBus, ILogger — and six more. Each describes a capability, not a provider. All 12 converge in a single PlatformContainer — the only gateway through which plugins access infrastructure.

principles.title: Level the playing field
principles.body: Enterprise-grade internal developer platforms shouldn't require an enterprise budget. Any team — one person or a hundred — should be able to run AI orchestration, workflow automation, and a proper API gateway.

principles.title: Plugins, not features
principles.body: Everything optional lives in a plugin. No base classes, no framework inheritance — just a typed contract and a manifest file. If you can write a module, you can write a plugin.

principles.title: Observability by default
principles.body: Software you cannot inspect is software you cannot trust. Every workflow run, every agent call, every state mutation is traceable without attaching a debugger or adding extra tooling.

origin: In 2025, the CTO at my company rolled out AI code review for the backend team. As the frontend lead, I disagreed — with both the tool and the approach. I built an MVP AI Review for frontend and backend together overnight. We discussed the architecture the next day; half my proposals made it in. I wanted more.

I published the first version on GitHub as open source. Then I wanted something bigger — not just a review tool, but real infrastructure for AI tooling. Step by step it grew: workflow engine, plugin system, AI gateway.

Today KB Labs is 145+ packages, its own release pipeline, and Go binaries for building and managing services. Public release is summer 2026. Source is open — `@kb-labs/review` is one of the first plugins.

meta.title: Security
meta.description: On-prem by default, open source, consent-first data sharing, plugin sandboxing, and JWT-based Gateway authentication.

hero.title: Your code and data stay on your machine.
hero.subtitle: KB Labs is on-prem first, open source end-to-end, and never sends data without your explicit consent. Verify everything yourself in the source.

pillars.title: On-prem by default
pillars.description: KB Labs runs entirely on your infrastructure. No cloud dependency, no data leaving your network. You control where your code, workflow state, and logs are stored.

pillars.title: Consent-first data sharing
pillars.description: Nothing is sent externally without explicit user consent. The CLI installer asks before every network call. Demo mode offers three choices: gateway proxy, local only, or bring your own API key.

pillars.title: Open source & auditable
pillars.description: The entire platform is open source. Every line of code is auditable. No black boxes, no hidden telemetry, no trust-us-it's-safe. Verify yourself.

practices.title: Plugin sandboxing
practices.description: Plugins declare capabilities upfront in their manifest. The runtime enforces boundaries — a plugin cannot access resources outside its declared scope. Execution modes include in-process and subprocess isolation.

practices.title: Gateway authentication
practices.description: The Gateway uses JWT-based authentication with device-scoped tokens. Credentials are generated per device, stored locally (file permissions 0600), and never shared across users.

practices.title: No telemetry without opt-in
practices.description: CLI telemetry is disabled by default. When enabled, we collect only anonymous usage statistics (OS, version, install duration). No source code, file names, or credentials are ever collected.

practices.title: Dependency management
practices.description: Every dependency is pinned and checked on every build across the entire monorepo: import analysis, export analysis, duplicate detection — all automated through DevKit tooling we use ourselves.

practices.title: Config file security
practices.description: Platform configuration files containing credentials are written with restricted permissions (0600). API keys and secrets are never logged or included in telemetry events.

practices.title: Demo mode transparency
practices.description: In demo mode, only git diffs are sent for AI review — never full source code. The Gateway is a pass-through proxy: no content is stored or logged. Only token counts are tracked for rate limiting.

dpa.title: Data Processing Agreement
dpa.intro: This Data Processing Agreement ("DPA") supplements our Terms of Service and applies where KB Labs processes personal data on your behalf as a data processor under GDPR or equivalent data protection law.

sections.title: Definitions
items: Controller — you, the customer, who determines the purposes and means of processing.
Processor — KB Labs, acting on your instructions to process personal data.
Sub-processor — a third party engaged by KB Labs to assist in processing.
Personal data — any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person.

sections.title: Processing instructions
sections.body: KB Labs will process personal data only on your documented instructions, including those set out in the Terms of Service and this DPA, unless required to do so by applicable law. KB Labs will promptly inform you if any instruction infringes data protection law.

sections.title: Confidentiality
sections.body: KB Labs will ensure that persons authorised to process personal data are subject to appropriate confidentiality obligations and are trained in data protection requirements.

sections.title: Security measures
sections.body: KB Labs implements and maintains appropriate technical and organisational security measures, including encryption at rest (AES-256), encryption in transit (TLS 1.3), access controls, and regular security reviews. See our Security page for details.

sections.title: Sub-processors

items: Cloud infrastructure — hosting, storage, and networking
Payment processing — Stripe (PCI DSS Level 1 certified)

sections.note: We will notify you of any intended changes to sub-processors with at least 14 days' notice. A current list of sub-processors is available on request at kirillBaranovJob@yandex.ru.

sections.title: International transfers
sections.body: Where personal data is transferred outside the EEA or UK, KB Labs ensures appropriate safeguards are in place, such as Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or adequacy decisions. Enterprise customers may request EU-only data residency.

sections.title: Data subject rights

sections.title: Data breach notification

Notification will include the nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of affected data subjects, and recommended mitigation steps.

cookies.title: Cookie Policy
cookies.intro: This Cookie Policy explains what cookies KB Labs uses, why we use them, and how you can control them.

sections.title: What are cookies?
sections.body: Cookies are small text files stored on your device by your browser when you visit a website. They help websites remember your preferences and session state between page loads.

sections.title: What we use
sections.body: We use three categories of cookies:
items: Essential — required for the platform to work. Cannot be disabled. These include session authentication and CSRF protection.
Functional — remember your preferences (theme, sidebar state). Disabling these means your preferences reset on each visit.
Analytics — aggregated, anonymised usage data to help us improve the product. No personal data is included. Can be opted out below.

rows.name: kb_session
rows.purpose: Authenticates your session after login. Required for the platform to function.

rows.name: kb_csrf
rows.purpose: CSRF protection token. Prevents cross-site request forgery attacks.

rows.name: kb_prefs
rows.purpose: Stores UI preferences such as sidebar state and theme selection.

rows.name: _analytics
rows.purpose: Aggregated, anonymised usage analytics. Used to understand which features are used most.

sections.title: Third-party cookies
sections.body: We do not use third-party advertising cookies or social media tracking pixels. Our analytics is self-hosted and does not share data with external analytics providers.

sections.title: How to control cookies
sections.body: You can control cookies in two ways:
items: Browser settings — all modern browsers allow you to block or delete cookies. Note that blocking essential cookies will prevent you from logging in.
Account settings — opt out of analytics cookies in your account preferences.
sections.note: For browser-specific instructions, refer to your browser's help documentation.

sections.title: Changes
sections.body: We'll update this page if we add new cookies. Material changes will be notified via email.

sections.body: Questions? Email kirillBaranovJob@yandex.ru.

hero.subtitle: Concrete things teams automate with KB Labs today — not abstract promises.

items.title: Release Orchestration
items.hook: Your releases live in five Slack threads, three scripts, and a prayer.
items.situation: Every release is a manual coordination exercise — CI jobs, chat messages, local scripts, people who might be on leave.
items.how: tests → checks → AI review → QA gate → final approve. One declarative workflow. Plugins enforce each step.
items.result: Release cycle: days → minutes. Every step traced. No one skips the gate.

items.title: QA Regressions and Trends
items.hook: You find out about the regression after the customer does.
items.situation: Quality signals are scattered — lint here, tests there, trends nowhere. Issues surface post-release when remediation costs the most.
items.how: A QA plugin runs recurring checks on every change, tracks package-level trends, and surfaces degradation signals before they ship.
items.result: Catch regressions at commit time, not in production. Remediation cost drops significantly.

items.title: Commit and Change Policy
items.hook: Your commit history is a graveyard of 'fix', 'wip', and 'asdf'.
items.situation: Commit discipline lives in a Confluence page nobody reads. Enforcement depends on who reviews and whether they care that day.
items.how: A commit plugin enforces policy at the workflow level — message format, scope, type — declared once, applied everywhere.
items.result: Consistent change history across the team. Fewer manual fixups before release.

items.title: AI Review Gate
items.hook: Code review is your slowest and most inconsistent step.
items.situation: Review throughput is a bottleneck. Quality bar varies by reviewer, by day, by team. Some PRs get ten comments, others slip through.
items.how: AI review runs as a required pipeline stage with declarative policy — not a suggestion, a gate. Configurable per repo.
items.result: Faster cycle times. A consistent quality baseline that doesn't depend on who's available.

items.title: Monorepo Dependency Ops
items.hook: Your monorepo changes are a game of 'hope nothing breaks'.
items.situation: Dependency updates, package sequencing, and governance live in ad-hoc scripts and tribal knowledge. Every big change is a gamble.
items.how: Plugins handle dependency operations, build sequencing, and package governance — declaratively, with change tracking.
items.result: Predictable monorepo changes. CI glue code replaced by observable, repeatable workflows.

items.title: Internal Agent Workflows
items.hook: Your AI agents are ungoverned scripts running on someone's laptop.
items.situation: Agent scenarios are bolted onto separate stacks with no unified policy, no audit trail, and no way to enforce security boundaries.
items.how: Agents run as plugins inside the same policy-first runtime as your standard workflows — same governance, same observability.
items.result: One controlled system for agent automation. No separate stack to maintain or secure.

meta.title: kb-deploy — docker compose, but for prod
meta.description: docker compose on steroids for production: git-aware affected detection, multi-host SSH deploys, build → push → pull → restart in one command, stateful infra in the same file.

hero.title: Ship to prod before Kubernetes is worth it.
hero.description: docker compose, but for prod — git-aware, multi-host, SSH-first. Build, push, pull, restart — in the right order, only what changed. One binary, one config, no cluster to babysit.

compareCompose.title: If you know docker compose, you already know kb-deploy.
compareCompose.description: Same mental model, same YAML shape, same up/down rhythm — but extended with the things compose intentionally doesn't do.

rows.kbDeploy: One command, over SSH, from your laptop
git diff against last deployed SHA
Targets can live on different hosts in one file
One run command, verified at each step
infra up / down / status, idempotent, tracked
status shows SHA drift per target

items.scenario: You have three services on one VPS. To deploy, you SSH in, git pull, rebuild an image, docker compose up -d, then watch the logs and hope the new image actually came up. Every time.
items.fix: kb-deploy run. Images built, pushed, pulled, restarted — in the right order, with health verified before moving on.

items.scenario: You changed one service but your deploy script rebuilds all of them, so a 2-minute fix takes 15 minutes to ship. Or you rebuild nothing and bring the wrong SHA to prod.
items.fix: kb-deploy diffs against git and only touches the targets that actually changed. Everything else stays put.

items.scenario: Half your infra — the database, the cache, the broker — lives outside your app. Nothing manages them together. You bring them up with ad-hoc compose files that nobody maintains.
items.fix: kb-deploy infra up brings the stateful layer up from the same deploy.yaml. One file, idempotent, no drift.

config.title: One file. Apps and infra together.
config.description: Put .kb/deploy.yaml in your repo. kb-deploy reads it and figures out what to build, what to push, and what to restart — over SSH, on the target host.
config.note: Same file is read by kb-monitor afterwards. Deploy and observe from one source of truth.
config.example:
registry: ghcr.io/your-org

targets:
  api:
    path: apps/api
    dockerfile: Dockerfile
    host: prod-1
    compose: infra/compose/api.yml
    health: http://localhost:3000/health

  web:
    path: apps/web
    host: prod-1
    compose: infra/compose/web.yml

infrastructure:
  postgres:
    host: prod-1
    image: postgres:16
    volumes:
      - /data/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    host: prod-1
    image: redis:7

meta.title: kb-monitor — Set it once, let the agent watch
meta.description: Agent-native observability for services deployed via kb-deploy. Health, uptime, image SHAs, logs, exec — all with --json, so an agent can watch your services while you forget monitoring is a task.

hero.title: Set it once. Let the agent watch.
hero.description: kb-monitor observes services deployed via kb-deploy. Every command has --json with a stable schema, so an agent can poll health, read logs, and react — while you stop thinking about monitoring as a task you own.

agentWatchdog.title: Monitoring as something your agent does.
agentWatchdog.description: Every kb-monitor command outputs structured JSON with a stable, documented schema. Point an agent at it on a schedule and monitoring stops being a human task — it becomes a loop that reacts on its own.
agentWatchdog.codeExample:
# run every 5 minutes via cron
kb-monitor status --json > /tmp/state.json

# the agent reads state, reasons over it, and reacts:
#   - health failing on kb-labs-web → kb-deploy run kb-labs-web
#   - new SHA in prod → post to #releases
#   - container restarting → fetch logs and open an issue
#
# you wrote the rules once. you never open a terminal again.
agentWatchdog.note: Same pattern works with any agent runtime — Claude, Codex, your own plugin-agent running inside KB Labs. The stable schema is the contract.

items.scenario: Something is wrong in prod. You SSH into the host, docker ps, grep for the container, docker logs, scroll through a wall of text, forget the flag for tail, try again. Ten minutes before you've seen a single relevant line.
items.fix: kb-monitor logs <target> --follow. Tailing logs from your laptop, no SSH session, no container name to remember.

items.scenario: A service went down at 3am. You want to know: is it alive now? When did it come back? What image SHA is it on? You run three different commands against the host to piece it together.
items.fix: kb-monitor status shows every target in one table: running state, uptime, image SHA — ready to paste into an incident channel.

items.scenario: Your agent wrote a hotfix and deployed it. Now it needs to verify the deploy actually went through and the service is healthy. Without structured output it has to parse docker ps output.
items.fix: Every command supports --json with a stable schema. Agents get a clean dataset to reason over — no screen-scraping.

features.title: How it works
features.description: The things that matter once your services are running.

list.title: Health across every target, one command
list.description: health runs HTTP or TCP probes against every target defined in deploy.yaml. Pass or fail per service, with latency.

list.title: Running state, uptime, and image SHA
list.description: status gives you the three things you always want during an incident — current state, how long it's been that way, and exactly what's running.

list.title: Remote logs without SSH sessions
list.description: logs streams docker logs from the host. --lines for a tail, --follow for live tail. No more SSH into a box just to tail a file.

items.scenario: You run make dev, everything looks like it started, but the API crashes immediately. Postgres wasn't up yet. You re-run. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't.

config.title: One file, any project.
config.description: Drop devservices.yaml in your project root. kb-dev finds it automatically by walking up from the current directory.
config.note: KB Labs projects use .kb/devservices.yaml — same format, different location.
config.example:
name: my-project

services:
  postgres:
    type: docker
    command: docker run --rm -p 5432:5432 postgres:16
    health_check: http://localhost:5432
    port: 5432

  api:
    command: pnpm dev
    port: 3000
    health_check: http://localhost:3000/health
    depends_on: [postgres]

  worker:
    command: pnpm worker
    depends_on: [postgres]

features.title: How it works

list.title: Knows when a service actually died
list.description: Tracks the real PID because it spawned the process. No port scanning, no stale state, no guessing.

list.title: Doesn't call it alive until it actually responds
list.description: HTTP, TCP, and command health probes with response latency. Reports alive only after the check passes.

list.title: Starts postgres before the thing that needs postgres
list.description: Topological sort across your services. Parallel start within each layer, correct order guaranteed.

list.title: Brings it back without you watching the terminal
list.description: Watchdog with exponential backoff. A crashed service restarts automatically, up to 5 retries.

list.title: Stops the whole tree, not just the parent
list.description: Uses process group kill to stop the entire subtree. No orphaned child processes left behind.

list.title: Every command is scriptable
list.description: Every command outputs structured JSON with an ok field. Failures include a hint with the exact fix. Works in CI, Makefiles, and agent workflows.

meta.title: kb-devkit — Build monorepos that scale
meta.description: Go binary that runs build, lint, test, and deploy across hundreds of packages with content-addressable caching. Identical inputs skip execution. Works with any pnpm monorepo.

hero.title: Build 100 packages in the time it used to take to build 10.
hero.description: One binary that caches task outputs by input hash, runs dependent tasks in the right order, and only reruns what actually changed.

items.scenario: You change one type in a shared package and rebuild the whole monorepo. 10 minutes later you find out the type was wrong. You do it again.
items.fix: kb-devkit hashes your inputs. Change one package, only that package and its dependents rebuild.

items.scenario: CI takes 20 minutes on every PR. Nobody knows which packages actually changed. You end up building everything because it's safer.
items.fix: kb-devkit run build --affected finds exactly what changed via git diff and BFS through the dependency graph.

items.scenario: You're running lint, type-check, and tests in parallel but the type-check needs the dist/ from a dep that hasn't built yet. Errors that aren't real.
items.fix: deps: ["^build"] in devkit.yaml. Deps' build finishes before your task starts. Guaranteed.

config.title: One file, any monorepo.
config.description: Drop devkit.yaml in your workspace root. Declare tasks once, run them everywhere. kb-devkit finds packages by glob patterns and applies the right rules.
config.note: The KB Labs workspace uses kb-devkit for all 125+ packages across 18 repos.
config.example:
tasks:
  build:
    command: tsup
    inputs:
      - "src/**"
      - "tsconfig*.json"
    outputs:
      - "dist/**"
    deps:
      - "^build"  # deps first

  lint:
    command: eslint src/
    inputs: ["src/**"]

  test:
    command: vitest run
    inputs: ["src/**", "test/**"]
    deps: ["build"]

affected:
  strategy: submodules

features.title: How it works
features.description: The things that matter once the pain is familiar.

list.title: Identical inputs, zero work
list.description: SHA256 hash of all input files. Cache hit restores outputs in ~1ms. No rebuild, no rerun, no wait.

list.title: Runs in the right order
list.description: DAG across all (package × task) pairs. Packages with no interdependencies run in parallel. deps: ["^build"] means what it says.

list.title: Knows what actually changed
list.description: git diff inside each submodule, BFS through the reverse dependency graph. --affected targets only what needs to run.

list.title: Works with any task
list.description: build, lint, test, type-check, deploy, or anything with a shell command. cache: false for side-effectful tasks.

list.title: Quality checks built in
list.description: Validates tsconfig inheritance, eslint config, required scripts, dep consistency. kb-devkit check catches drift before CI does.

list.title: Every command is scriptable
list.description: All commands output structured JSON with an ok field. Results include per-package stdout, stderr, exit code, and duration.

list.title: Docker build context pruning
list.description: kb-devkit bundle resolves the full workspace:* closure and emits only the packages needed to build the target. Equivalent to turbo prune — drop in before pnpm install in your Dockerfile.

commands.title: Commands
commands.description: All commands support --json for CI and agent workflows.

list.description: Run build across all packages in dependency order. Cached results restore in ~1ms.
Run only on packages changed relative to HEAD, plus all their downstream dependents.
Chain multiple tasks. Each respects its own inputs, outputs, and dep ordering.
Target specific packages by name. Useful for focused rebuilds during development.
Validate all packages against devkit.yaml rules: naming, tsconfig, eslint, deps, structure.
Workspace health score (A–F), issue counts by category, coverage metrics.
Auto-fix safe violations found by check. Use --dry-run to preview first.
Check staged files only. Exits with code 1 on violations. For pre-commit hooks.

Prune the monorepo to a minimal Docker build context. Resolves transitive workspace:* deps, emits pruned pnpm-workspace.yaml and package.json files. --docker splits into json/ (deps layer) and full/ (build layer).
